clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman presents with a 10-day history of purulent nasal discharge, right-sided facial pain, and nasal congestion. She reports that symptoms were improving after 5 days but have worsened over the past 2 days. Temperature is 38.2°C. On examination there is tenderness over the right maxillary sinus. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial antibiotic choice?
options: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg orally twice daily for 5–7 days
Amoxicillin 500 mg orally three times daily for 10–14 days
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally twice daily for 7 days
Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 10 days
Azithromycin 500 mg orally once daily for 3 days
explanation: This patient has acute bacterial rhinosinusitis (ABRS) evidenced by symptoms lasting >10 days with a 'double-sickening' pattern (initial improvement then worsening). Amoxicillin-clavulanate is the first-line antibiotic of choice because it covers Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, and Moraxella catarrhalis — including beta-lactamase-producing strains — which are the predominant pathogens in ABRS.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — amoxicillin-clavulanate is first-line for ABRS due to its broad coverage including beta-lactamase-producing organisms.
Amoxicillin alone is no longer recommended as first-line because it does not cover beta-lactamase-producing H. influenzae and M. catarrhalis, which are common causes of ABRS.
Ciprofloxacin has poor coverage for S. pneumoniae and is reserved for penicillin-allergic patients or treatment failure; it is not first-line for ABRS.
Doxycycline is an alternative for penicillin-allergic patients but is not the preferred first-line agent; it has inconsistent activity against H. influenzae.
Azithromycin has limited efficacy against S. pneumoniae due to high macrolide resistance rates and is not recommended as first-line for ABRS.
clinicalPearl: The 'double-sickening' pattern — initial improvement followed by worsening — is a key criterion for diagnosing acute bacterial (rather than viral) rhinosinusitis and justifies antibiotic therapy.
keyClue: 10-day symptom duration with double-sickening pattern and fever indicates bacterial rhinosinusitis requiring antibiotics.
commonExamTrap: Choosing amoxicillin alone instead of amoxicillin-clavulanate — the addition of clavulanate covers beta-lactamase-producing organisms that plain amoxicillin misses.
missedConcept: The distinction between viral and bacterial rhinosinusitis based on duration and pattern, and why clavulanate is added to amoxicillin for ABRS.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old man with a known history of allergic rhinitis presents with a 7-day history of nasal congestion, mucopurulent discharge, facial pressure, and hyposmia. He has not started any antibiotics. Nasal endoscopy reveals mucosal edema and purulent secretions in the middle meatus. In addition to saline nasal irrigation, which pharmacological agent is most likely to reduce symptom duration and severity in this patient?
options: Intranasal mometasone furoate spray
Oral prednisolone 30 mg daily for 5 days
Intranasal oxymetazoline spray for 10 days
Oral cetirizine 10 mg daily
Intranasal ipratropium bromide spray
explanation: Intranasal corticosteroids (INCS) such as mometasone furoate have evidence supporting their use as monotherapy or adjunct to antibiotics in acute rhinosinusitis — particularly in patients with underlying allergic rhinitis — by reducing mucosal inflammation and improving drainage. Guidelines recommend INCS for symptom relief in both viral and bacterial ARS, especially when allergic rhinitis is a predisposing factor.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — intranasal corticosteroids are evidence-based for symptom reduction in ARS, particularly with underlying allergic rhinitis.
Oral corticosteroids are not routinely recommended for uncomplicated ARS; the systemic side-effect burden is not justified when intranasal formulations are effective and safer.
Intranasal decongestants (oxymetazoline) provide short-term symptomatic relief but should not be used for more than 3–5 days due to risk of rhinitis medicamentosa; 10 days of use is inappropriate.
Antihistamines like cetirizine address allergic rhinitis symptoms but do not have established efficacy in reducing ARS duration or severity and are not specifically recommended for ARS.
Intranasal ipratropium reduces rhinorrhoea but does not address the inflammatory component or improve sinus drainage; it has no evidence base in ARS management.
clinicalPearl: Intranasal corticosteroids are the only pharmacological class with consistent evidence for symptom benefit in acute rhinosinusitis, especially in patients with concurrent allergic rhinitis.
keyClue: Known allergic rhinitis history combined with ARS findings points toward intranasal corticosteroids as the best adjunctive agent.
commonExamTrap: Prescribing oxymetazoline for 10 days — nasal decongestant sprays must be limited to 3–5 days maximum to avoid rebound congestion.
missedConcept: The role of INCS in ARS and the danger of prolonged topical decongestant use leading to rhinitis medicamentosa.

clinicalStem: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with a 3-day history of worsening right-sided facial pain and nasal congestion following a cold. Over the past 12 hours his parents noticed his right eye became swollen and he now has proptosis, restricted eye movements, and is febrile at 39.1°C. Visual acuity is reduced in the right eye. CT sinuses with contrast shows right ethmoid sinusitis with a rim-enhancing collection medial to the right orbit. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Emergency surgical drainage with intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics
Intravenous antibiotics alone with close monitoring every 6 hours
Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate and outpatient follow-up in 24 hours
Intranasal corticosteroids and nasal saline irrigation
Aspiration of the collection under local anaesthesia in the ENT clinic
explanation: This child has a subperiosteal orbital abscess (Chandler stage IV) complicating acute ethmoid sinusitis, evidenced by proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, reduced visual acuity, and a rim-enhancing collection on CT. Reduced visual acuity signals impending blindness from orbital apex syndrome or optic nerve compression — this is a surgical emergency requiring immediate drainage (endoscopic or external) combined with IV broad-spectrum antibiotics to preserve vision.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — subperiosteal abscess with reduced vision is a surgical emergency; IV antibiotics alone are insufficient once visual compromise is present.
IV antibiotics alone may be appropriate for preseptal cellulitis or early postseptal cellulitis without abscess and normal vision; reduced acuity mandates immediate surgical drainage.
Oral antibiotics with outpatient follow-up is entirely inappropriate for a child with orbital abscess and visual compromise — this risks permanent blindness.
Intranasal corticosteroids and saline are adjuncts for uncomplicated sinusitis; they have no role in managing an established orbital abscess with visual loss.
Clinic aspiration under local anaesthesia is inadequate for a child in an emergency setting with a well-defined abscess and visual compromise; formal surgical drainage is required.
clinicalPearl: Reduced visual acuity in a child with orbital cellulitis is an ophthalmic emergency — it indicates orbital abscess (Chandler stage IV) and mandates immediate surgical drainage to prevent permanent blindness.
keyClue: Proptosis + restricted eye movements + reduced visual acuity + rim-enhancing orbital collection = subperiosteal abscess requiring surgical emergency.
commonExamTrap: Choosing IV antibiotics alone — this is only appropriate for preseptal cellulitis or mild postseptal inflammation without visual compromise.
missedConcept: The Chandler classification of orbital complications of sinusitis and the threshold for surgical intervention when visual acuity is affected.
redFlagReason: Reduced visual acuity from orbital abscess indicates impending blindness — delayed surgery risks permanent loss of vision in a child.

topic: Long-term management of chronic rhinosinusitis without polyps
clinicalStem: A 45-year-old male accountant presents with a 14-week history of nasal obstruction, mucopurulent discharge, facial pressure, and reduced smell. He denies asthma or aspirin sensitivity. CT sinuses shows bilateral maxillary and ethmoid mucosal thickening without polyps. He has tried two courses of antibiotics without sustained improvement. Which of the following represents the most appropriate long-term pharmacological strategy?
options: High-volume low-pressure nasal saline irrigation plus topical intranasal corticosteroids for at least 12 weeks
Continuous low-dose oral doxycycline for 12 weeks
Long-term oral prednisolone 10 mg daily
Oral antihistamine plus intranasal decongestant spray
Montelukast 10 mg daily alone
explanation: Chronic rhinosinusitis without nasal polyps (CRSsNP) is managed first-line with combined high-volume saline irrigation (for mucociliary clearance and mechanical lavage) and intranasal corticosteroids for at least 12 weeks, as per EPOS guidelines. This approach addresses both the inflammatory component and improves mucociliary function without systemic corticosteroid side effects.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — EPOS guidelines endorse high-volume saline irrigation plus INCS for at least 12 weeks as the cornerstone of CRSsNP management.
Long-term doxycycline has been studied mainly in CRS with polyps for its anti-inflammatory properties; it is not a first-line recommended strategy for CRSsNP without polyps.
Long-term oral prednisolone carries significant systemic risks (adrenal suppression, osteoporosis, metabolic effects) and is not appropriate as primary long-term therapy for CRS without polyps.
Antihistamines target allergic rhinitis symptoms; they are not effective for CRS itself. Intranasal decongestants are harmful with prolonged use and not recommended in CRS management.
Montelukast has a role in aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease and CRS with polyps but lacks sufficient evidence as monotherapy for CRSsNP and is not a guideline-endorsed first-line agent.
clinicalPearl: High-volume saline irrigation combined with intranasal corticosteroids for a minimum of 12 weeks is the EPOS-recommended first-line medical treatment for chronic rhinosinusitis without nasal polyps.
keyClue: 14 weeks of symptoms, two failed antibiotic courses, no polyps on CT — points to CRSsNP requiring sustained anti-inflammatory topical therapy.
commonExamTrap: Reaching for long-term oral steroids as first-line — these are reserved for short rescue courses in refractory cases and carry unacceptable long-term risks.
missedConcept: The EPOS step-up approach to CRSsNP and why saline irrigation is a critical, not optional, component of first-line therapy.

topic: Medical management of nasal polyps — oral corticosteroids
clinicalStem: A 52-year-old woman with known CRS and bilateral nasal polyps (visible below the inferior turbinate on anterior rhinoscopy) presents with near-complete nasal obstruction and anosmia, significantly affecting her quality of life. She has been on intranasal fluticasone for 6 months with only partial relief. Examination confirms large bilateral polyps. No contraindications to systemic steroids are identified. What is the most appropriate next step in pharmacological management?
options: A short course of oral prednisolone (medical polypectomy) followed by maintenance intranasal corticosteroids
Switch intranasal fluticasone to mometasone furoate
Add intranasal ipratropium bromide spray
Start long-term low-dose oral prednisolone 5 mg daily indefinitely
Prescribe an oral antihistamine and refer for allergy testing before any further treatment
explanation: When large nasal polyps persist despite adequate INCS therapy, a short course of systemic oral corticosteroids ('medical polypectomy') is recommended to rapidly shrink polyp bulk, relieve obstruction, and restore olfaction. This is followed by long-term maintenance with intranasal corticosteroids to prevent recurrence. This approach is endorsed by EPOS guidelines before considering surgery.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — a short oral steroid course is the appropriate step-up when INCS alone fails to control large symptomatic polyps, acting as a medical polypectomy.

Switching between intranasal corticosteroid molecules is unlikely to produce meaningful benefit when 6 months of fluticasone has provided only partial relief — a different class of intervention (systemic steroids) is needed.
Intranasal ipratropium controls rhinorrhoea but has no meaningful effect on polyp size or nasal obstruction.

Indefinite low-dose oral prednisolone exposes the patient to long-term steroid complications (osteoporosis, adrenal suppression, diabetes) and is not guideline-recommended; short courses are used instead.

While allergy evaluation may be relevant, delaying treatment with an antihistamine while awaiting allergy testing ignores the immediate burden of significant obstruction and anosmia that requires prompt pharmacological step-up.
clinicalPearl: Oral corticosteroids used as a short-course 'medical polypectomy' can rapidly reduce large polyp burden — always follow with maintenance INCS to delay or prevent recurrence.
keyClue: Large bilateral polyps with anosmia, visible below turbinate after 6 months of INCS — inadequate control with topical treatment alone warrants systemic escalation.
commonExamTrap: Switching between INCS molecules rather than escalating to oral steroids when topical therapy has failed — this delays effective treatment.
missedConcept: The concept of 'medical polypectomy' with systemic corticosteroids as a step-up before surgery in nasal polyposis.

topic: Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease and nasal polyps
clinicalStem: A 47-year-old male with bilateral nasal polyps, asthma, and a prior episode of severe bronchospasm after taking ibuprofen for knee pain presents to your ENT clinic for follow-up. He asks what he should tell doctors and dentists regarding pain relief. Which of the following statements about medication use is most accurate for this patient?
options: He should avoid all NSAIDs and aspirin; paracetamol (acetaminophen) at standard doses is safe, and selective COX-2 inhibitors may be used cautiously with monitoring
He should avoid only aspirin; ibuprofen is safe because it is a different drug class
He may take ibuprofen safely as long as he pre-medicates with a bronchodilator
All NSAIDs including COX-2 inhibitors are absolutely contraindicated and paracetamol must also be avoided
He should undergo aspirin desensitisation before using any analgesic
explanation: This patient has Samter's triad (aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease: nasal polyps, asthma, and NSAID sensitivity). All non-selective NSAIDs and aspirin are contraindicated due to COX-1 inhibition causing leukotriene overproduction. Paracetamol at standard doses (up to 1g) is generally safe as it minimally inhibits COX-1. Selective COX-2 inhibitors may be used with caution under monitoring as they do not significantly inhibit COX-1.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — all non-selective NSAIDs are contraindicated; paracetamol at standard doses is safe; COX-2 inhibitors can be cautiously used.
Ibuprofen is a non-selective COX-1/COX-2 inhibitor — it is equally contraindicated as aspirin in AERD and caused the prior bronchospasm episode.
Pre-medicating with a bronchodilator does not prevent the leukotriene-mediated reaction triggered by COX-1 inhibition; NSAIDs remain dangerous in this patient.

COX-2 inhibitors are generally well tolerated in AERD because they do not significantly inhibit COX-1; absolute contraindication of all NSAIDs including COX-2 inhibitors is an overstatement. Paracetamol at standard doses is also safe.
Aspirin desensitisation is a therapeutic option but is not required before using analgesics — the immediate advice is to use safe alternatives (paracetamol/COX-2 inhibitors) and avoid NSAIDs.
clinicalPearl: In Samter's triad (AERD), avoid all non-selective NSAIDs; paracetamol at doses ≤1 g is safe, and COX-2 inhibitors may be used cautiously — COX-1 inhibition is the mechanism that triggers leukotriene-mediated reactions.
keyClue: Triad of nasal polyps + asthma + prior NSAID-induced bronchospasm = Samter's triad / AERD.
commonExamTrap: Thinking only aspirin needs to be avoided — all non-selective NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenac) are equally dangerous in AERD.
missedConcept: The COX-1 inhibition mechanism behind AERD and the safety profile of paracetamol and COX-2 inhibitors as alternatives.

topic: Pharmacological treatment of allergic rhinitis — stepped approach
clinicalStem: A 22-year-old female university student presents with a 3-year history of sneezing, watery rhinorrhoea, nasal itch, and bilateral nasal congestion throughout the year. Skin-prick testing is positive to house dust mite. She tried loratadine 10 mg once daily for 4 weeks with partial relief of sneezing but persistent congestion. She does not have eye symptoms. What is the most effective pharmacological upgrade for this patient?
options: Add intranasal corticosteroid spray (e.g., fluticasone propionate) as it is more effective than antihistamines for nasal congestion in persistent allergic rhinitis
Switch loratadine to a first-generation antihistamine such as chlorphenamine
Add oral montelukast as monotherapy
Add intranasal cromoglicate spray
Start sublingual immunotherapy without any other medication changes
explanation: Intranasal corticosteroids (INCS) are superior to oral antihistamines for treating nasal congestion in moderate-to-severe persistent allergic rhinitis. When an antihistamine alone provides inadequate control — particularly of congestion — guidelines recommend adding or switching to an INCS. Multiple randomised trials and meta-analyses confirm INCS superiority over antihistamines for overall symptom control.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — INCS are the most effective single pharmacological agent for allergic rhinitis, particularly for congestion, and outperform oral antihistamines.

First-generation antihistamines (e.g., chlorphenamine) cause significant sedation and anticholinergic effects; switching to a sedating agent is not an upgrade when congestion is the primary uncontrolled symptom.
Montelukast has modest evidence in allergic rhinitis but is less effective than INCS for nasal congestion; it is not the most effective upgrade as monotherapy.
Intranasal cromoglicate is a mast cell stabiliser with modest efficacy and requires frequent dosing (4–6 times daily); it is inferior to INCS for persistent moderate-to-severe allergic rhinitis.

Sublingual immunotherapy (SLIT) is appropriate for patients inadequately controlled on pharmacotherapy but is not the immediate pharmacological upgrade — medication optimisation should precede allergen immunotherapy.
clinicalPearl: Intranasal corticosteroids are the single most effective pharmacological treatment for allergic rhinitis across all symptom domains — they are superior to antihistamines, especially for nasal congestion.
keyClue: Persistent congestion not controlled by antihistamine alone in house-dust-mite-sensitive patient — INCS is the most effective next step.
commonExamTrap: Adding a sedating first-generation antihistamine rather than an INCS — sedating antihistamines are a downgrade and do not address congestion more effectively.
missedConcept: The pharmacological hierarchy in allergic rhinitis: INCS > antihistamines for congestion and overall symptom control.

subtopic: Indications and prevention of sensitisation progression
clinicalStem: A 16-year-old boy has had seasonal allergic rhinitis to grass pollen for 4 years, with moderate-to-severe symptoms each spring despite using intranasal fluticasone and oral cetirizine. He has no asthma currently but his spirometry shows a mild reversible obstructive pattern. His mother asks whether any treatment could change the long-term trajectory of his allergy. Which treatment option has the strongest evidence for disease modification — preventing progression to asthma and reducing the development of new sensitivities?
options: Subcutaneous allergen immunotherapy (SCIT) with standardised grass pollen extract
Increasing intranasal fluticasone to maximum licensed dose
Adding oral montelukast throughout the pollen season
Prescribing a regular inhaled corticosteroid prophylactically during pollen season
Switching cetirizine to fexofenadine for better tolerability
explanation: Subcutaneous allergen immunotherapy (SCIT) — and sublingual immunotherapy (SLIT) — are the only treatments with proven disease-modifying effects in allergic rhinitis. Randomised controlled trials demonstrate that SCIT reduces the risk of developing asthma in rhinitis patients, decreases the risk of new sensitisations, and provides prolonged symptom control after a 3-year course. No pharmacological agent achieves this disease-modifying effect.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — allergen immunotherapy (SCIT or SLIT) is the only disease-modifying treatment for allergic rhinitis, with evidence for preventing asthma progression and new sensitisations.
Higher-dose INCS controls symptoms but does not alter the underlying allergic response or prevent sensitisation to new allergens or asthma development.
Montelukast has symptomatic benefit especially for ocular symptoms and as an add-on, but it is not disease-modifying and does not prevent asthma development or new sensitisations.
Prophylactic ICS during pollen season treats bronchial inflammation but does not modify the allergic march or prevent new sensitisations; it does not address the underlying immunological mechanism.
Switching antihistamines improves tolerability but has no impact on disease course, asthma risk, or sensitisation progression.
clinicalPearl: Allergen immunotherapy (SCIT/SLIT) is the only disease-modifying treatment for allergic rhinitis — it reduces the risk of progression to asthma and development of new allergen sensitivities.
keyClue: Adolescent with pollen-triggered rhinitis, mild reversible obstruction, asking about long-term disease modification — points to allergen immunotherapy.
commonExamTrap: Recommending maximum INCS dose as the disease-modifying option — INCS controls symptoms but does not alter the natural history of allergic disease.
missedConcept: The distinction between symptomatic pharmacotherapy (which controls symptoms) and disease-modifying treatment (allergen immunotherapy, which alters the allergic immune response).

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of perennial watery rhinorrhoea, predominantly triggered by cold air, spicy food, strong smells, and changes in weather. She has no sneezing, nasal itch, or eye symptoms. Skin-prick tests are negative to a full panel of aeroallergens. Serum total IgE is normal. Nasal endoscopy shows pale-pink mucosa without polyps. Which pharmacological agent is most specifically targeted at the predominant mechanism of her rhinorrhoea?
options: Intranasal ipratropium bromide spray
Intranasal fluticasone propionate
Oral loratadine 10 mg daily
Intranasal azelastine
Oral pseudoephedrine
explanation: This patient has vasomotor (non-allergic, non-infective) rhinitis characterised by watery rhinorrhoea provoked by non-immunological stimuli without allergic features. The predominant mechanism is parasympathetic (cholinergic) hypersensitivity producing excessive glandular secretion. Intranasal ipratropium bromide, an anticholinergic agent, directly blocks muscarinic receptors in nasal glands and is the most targeted pharmacological treatment for the rhinorrhoea of vasomotor rhinitis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — intranasal ipratropium bromide targets the cholinergic mechanism of watery rhinorrhoea in vasomotor rhinitis and is the most specifically indicated agent.
Intranasal fluticasone is effective for allergic rhinitis and CRS but has limited evidence for controlling rhinorrhoea in vasomotor rhinitis; it is not the most targeted agent for this mechanism.
Oral antihistamines (loratadine) are for IgE-mediated allergic rhinitis — this patient has normal IgE, negative skin tests, and no sneezing or itch, ruling out allergic rhinitis.

Intranasal azelastine has some evidence in non-allergic rhinitis but acts primarily as a histamine antagonist and mast-cell stabiliser; it is less specifically targeted at cholinergic rhinorrhoea than ipratropium.
Oral pseudoephedrine is a decongestant that addresses nasal congestion, not rhinorrhoea, and has cardiovascular side effects; it does not target the secretory mechanism.
clinicalPearl: Intranasal ipratropium bromide is the drug of choice for rhinorrhoea in vasomotor (non-allergic) rhinitis — it blocks parasympathetic overactivity driving excessive nasal gland secretion.
keyClue: Negative allergy tests + watery rhinorrhoea triggered by non-immunological stimuli (cold, spices, smells) = vasomotor rhinitis requiring anticholinergic therapy.
commonExamTrap: Prescribing an antihistamine for watery rhinorrhoea when allergy testing is negative — if there is no allergic component, antihistamines targeting histamine will be ineffective.
missedConcept: The cholinergic mechanism of vasomotor rhinitis and why an anticholinergic (ipratropium) is more targeted than antihistamines or steroids for this condition.

topic: Antibiotic use in chronic rhinosinusitis exacerbation
clinicalStem: A 60-year-old male smoker with a 2-year history of chronic rhinosinusitis without nasal polyps continues to have daily mucopurulent nasal discharge, postnasal drip, and facial pressure despite 3 months of intranasal budesonide and regular nasal saline irrigation. CT sinuses shows persistent bilateral maxillary and ethmoid mucosal thickening. He declines surgery. Nasal swab culture grows normal respiratory flora. Serum IgE is normal. Which pharmacological strategy has evidence for disease control in refractory CRSsNP when conventional therapy has failed and surgery is declined?
options: Long-term low-dose macrolide antibiotic (e.g., roxithromycin or azithromycin) for 12 weeks, utilising its anti-inflammatory rather than antibacterial properties
Continuous rotating courses of broad-spectrum antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate alternating with ciprofloxacin)
Long-term oral prednisolone 5 mg daily as maintenance anti-inflammatory therapy
Adding intranasal antihistamine (azelastine) to the current regimen
Prescribing nebulised tobramycin for biofilm eradication
explanation: Long-term low-dose macrolide therapy (e.g., roxithromycin 150 mg daily or azithromycin 250 mg three times weekly) for 12 weeks has Level Ib evidence in CRSsNP, particularly in patients with low IgE (non-Th2 phenotype) and normal culture results. The benefit is derived from macrolides' immunomodulatory and anti-biofilm properties (inhibiting neutrophil activation, IL-8, and biofilm formation) rather than antibacterial action — an effect well established also in diffuse panbronchiolitis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — low-dose macrolides for 12 weeks exploit immunomodulatory and anti-biofilm properties and have evidence in refractory CRSsNP with low IgE.
Continuous rotating broad-spectrum antibiotics promote resistance, do not address the inflammatory pathophysiology of CRS, and have no evidence base in refractory CRSsNP management.
Long-term oral prednisolone at any dose carries unacceptable systemic risks over months to years and is not appropriate as indefinite maintenance therapy for CRS.
Intranasal azelastine targets histamine-mediated allergic rhinitis; adding it to a CRS regimen in a patient with normal IgE does not address the underlying sinusitis pathophysiology.
Nebulised tobramycin is experimental and not a guideline-endorsed treatment for CRSsNP; there is insufficient evidence for routine use.
clinicalPearl: Low-dose macrolide antibiotics (12-week course) are an evidence-based option for refractory CRSsNP in patients with low serum IgE — the benefit is immunomodulatory, not antibacterial.
keyClue: Refractory CRSsNP, failed conventional therapy, normal IgE, declining surgery — macrolide immunomodulation is the evidence-based pharmacological option.
commonExamTrap: Using rotating broad-spectrum antibiotics — this promotes resistance and does not address the immune-dysregulation pathophysiology of chronic rhinosinusitis.
missedConcept: The immunomodulatory (non-antibacterial) mechanism of low-dose macrolides in CRS and their preferential benefit in the non-Th2 (low IgE) CRSsNP phenotype.

topic: Screening and early detection of nasal polyps
subtopic: Associations and screening in at-risk populations
clinicalStem: A paediatric pulmonologist refers a 14-year-old boy with known cystic fibrosis (CF) to ENT. He denies nasal symptoms but his CF nurse mentions he has never had a nasal assessment. Flexible nasal endoscopy reveals bilateral nasal polyps. His parents ask whether routine nasal endoscopy screening should be performed in children with CF even when they have no nasal complaints. Which statement best reflects the evidence and rationale for nasal screening in CF?
options: Yes — nasal polyps occur in up to 50% of CF patients and are frequently asymptomatic; routine endoscopic screening allows early detection and management before complications such as orbital or intracranial extension develop
No — nasal polyps in CF are always symptomatic and do not need screening; only symptomatic patients require endoscopy
No — nasal polyps in CF carry no clinical significance and never require treatment
Yes — but only if serum IgE is elevated, because CF-associated polyps are always allergic in origin
Screening is indicated only after the first episode of acute rhinosinusitis in CF patients
explanation: Nasal polyps develop in approximately 20–50% of patients with cystic fibrosis and are frequently asymptomatic, particularly in children who may not report nasal symptoms accurately. Because untreated large polyps can cause complications (including orbital expansion), and because sinonasal disease in CF contributes to lower respiratory tract exacerbations via aspiration of infected secretions, early detection through routine nasal endoscopy is advocated in CF care guidelines.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — polyps in CF are common (up to 50%) and often asymptomatic in children; routine endoscopic screening is warranted to detect and manage them early.
This is false — nasal polyps in CF are frequently asymptomatic, especially in paediatric patients, making symptom-based referral insufficient for detection.
CF nasal polyps require treatment when they are large or associated with complications; they are clinically significant and can contribute to lower respiratory tract disease and sinus complications.
CF nasal polyps are not IgE-mediated allergic polyps — they arise from abnormal chloride transport and dysfunctional mucociliary clearance; IgE level is not the screening criterion.
Waiting for an acute rhinosinusitis episode misses the window for early polyp detection — many CF patients develop polyps without ever presenting with overt sinusitis.
clinicalPearl: Nasal polyps occur in up to 50% of CF patients and are commonly asymptomatic in children — routine nasal endoscopy screening is recommended in CF care, regardless of nasal symptoms.
keyClue: Cystic fibrosis + asymptomatic child referred by pulmonologist + bilateral polyps found on endoscopy = the rationale for routine CF nasal screening.
commonExamTrap: Assuming CF polyps are always symptomatic and only investigating symptomatic patients — this misses the majority of CF-associated nasal polyps in paediatric patients.
missedConcept: The high prevalence of asymptomatic nasal polyps in CF and why routine sinonasal screening is a component of comprehensive CF care.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old man presents to the GP with a 4-month history of constant nasal obstruction. He admits to using oxymetazoline nasal spray several times daily for the past 5 months to relieve congestion. He initially started it during a common cold. On examination the nasal mucosa is erythematous and hyperaemic. He asks what he can use to treat his congestion. What is the best explanation for his current symptoms and the most appropriate management?
options: He has rhinitis medicamentosa from prolonged oxymetazoline use; management involves gradual withdrawal of the decongestant with intranasal corticosteroids to manage rebound congestion
He has developed chronic allergic rhinitis and requires long-term oral antihistamines
He has a deviated nasal septum uncovered by decongestant use; CT sinuses and surgical referral are indicated
He has developed bacterial sinusitis from the nasal spray; a course of antibiotics is required
He should switch to an intranasal antihistamine spray as a safer long-term alternative to oxymetazoline
explanation: Prolonged use of topical alpha-adrenergic decongestants such as oxymetazoline beyond 3–5 days causes rhinitis medicamentosa — rebound vasodilation and congestion as the nasal mucosa becomes dependent on the drug. Management involves stopping the offending decongestant (gradually or abruptly) while introducing intranasal corticosteroids to reduce rebound inflammation and ease withdrawal. Patient education about the maximum 3–5 day limit for topical decongestants is the key preventive measure.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — this is a classic presentation of rhinitis medicamentosa from prolonged oxymetazoline use; INCS-assisted decongestant withdrawal is the appropriate management.
There is no allergic history or trigger pattern; chronic allergic rhinitis does not develop from decongestant use, and antihistamines do not treat rebound congestion.

While a deviated septum is possible, the temporal relationship between starting oxymetazoline and worsening obstruction, combined with erythematous mucosa, strongly indicates rhinitis medicamentosa as the primary diagnosis.
Erythematous mucosa from alpha-agonist rebound does not indicate bacterial sinusitis; antibiotics are not indicated in the absence of purulent discharge, facial pain, or fever.
Intranasal antihistamines do not treat decongestant-rebound congestion; they target histamine-mediated rhinitis and would not be an appropriate substitute.
clinicalPearl: Topical nasal decongestants should never be used for more than 3–5 days — prolonged use causes rhinitis medicamentosa; treatment is decongestant withdrawal supported by intranasal corticosteroids.
keyClue: 5 months of oxymetazoline use, erythematous mucosa, and congestion that no longer responds — classic rhinitis medicamentosa from decongestant overuse.
commonExamTrap: Prescribing more or different decongestants rather than withdrawing the offending agent — this perpetuates the cycle of rebound congestion.
missedConcept: The mechanism and time limit of safe topical decongestant use; rhinitis medicamentosa is iatrogenic and preventable with proper patient counselling.
